one_line_takeaway: Token-drain attacks are real and cheap to build against production LLM agents (6-9x amplification on Gemini 2.5 Pro), but deployment realities -- emergent tool-composition workarounds, costly failure cascades, and interface-dependent visibility -- reshape their magnitude and stealth in ways constrained simulators cannot capture.
key_finding: In a real billed deployment, SVP achieves 6-7x token amplification while still returning correct answers (v1 ~6x, v2 ~7x), far below the 65-658x that prior simulator work [25] reported. Two deployment realities explain the gap: the authors used conservative parameters (L<=1000 vs. up to 15,000), and Gemini 2.5 Pro exhibited an EMERGENT scripting workaround at L=1000 -- it autonomously ran python3 to generate the sequence and reused $(cat /tmp/cal.txt), collapsing per-turn output from ~1000 tokens to a ~30-token shell command. The headline counterintuitive finding: the FAILED SVP v3 run (L=2000, T_max=5) consumed the MOST tokens (~249k context, ~9x, over 11 minutes) despite producing no correct result, because the agent entered an autonomous recovery cascade (fallback to benign skill, web search, killing stuck processes, retries) after classifying the skill's verification as 'faulty'. This challenges prior work's use of Attack Success Rate as a co-metric -- in deployed agents, the failure path can be strictly more expensive than the success path, so an attacker may deliberately set parameters above the compliance threshold. Stealth is shown to be interface-dependent, ranging from fully visible (GUI) to completely unobservable (cron/heartbeat).
core_contribution: The paper designs, implements, and evaluates Clawdrain, a Trojanized skill for the OpenClaw self-hosted agent framework that mounts a resource-amplification (token-exhaustion / economic denial-of-service) attack in a real, billed deployment rather than a simulator. Its own contributions are: (1) a concrete 'Segmented Verification Protocol' (SVP) malicious skill that forces multi-turn autoregressive generation of a full comma-separated integer 'calibration sequence' (length L, over T_max turns) via injected SKILL.md instructions plus a companion validation script returning PROGRESS/REPAIR/TERMINAL signals, while still returning a correct benign answer; (2) empirical amplification measurements against a production model (Gemini 2.5 Pro) across three SVP configurations, including the counterintuitive finding that a FAILED high-parameter configuration is the most expensive of all; (3) characterization of an emergent tool-composition 'defense' (the agent autonomously scripts its way around the drain) that reveals a simulator-vs-deployment gap; and (4) analysis of interface-dependent stealth (GUI vs. narrated TUI vs. autonomous cron/heartbeat) plus additional deployment-grounded vectors (input-token/SKILL.md bloat, persistent tool-output pollution, execution-frequency amplification, behavioral injection). The paper explicitly positions itself against, and contrasts with, prior work by Zhou et al. [25] ('Beyond Max Tokens'), which used a constrained simulator; that 65-658x result is cited comparison work, NOT this paper's own finding.
threat_model: Adversary can introduce a malicious/Trojanized skill into an OpenClaw deployment via four realistic channels: (1) publishing to the public ClawHub registry (stated to host 5,700+ one-command-installable third-party skills, indistinguishable from legitimate until manually audited); (2) compromising an upstream dependency of an otherwise-trusted skill; (3) insider/local file-system access to drop a skill into ~/.openclaw/skills/; or (4) inducing the agent to self-generate a malicious skill via prompt injection in a page/document. Adversary goal is economic DoS: maximize token consumption (which drives cost, latency, throughput) while preserving a correct final answer and plausibly-benign appearance, with a secondary goal of stealth against the user. The SKILL.md content is injected at the system-prompt privilege tier. Observability is modeled across three interfaces: Chat GUI (Telegram/Discord, all tool calls visible), narrated TUI (only summary narration shown), and autonomous cron/heartbeat (zero user-facing output). The agent is assumed to have general-purpose tools (shell/Python exec), which the paper shows can both blunt and amplify the attack.
limitations: Single target model (Gemini 2.5 Pro) and a single OpenClaw version (v2026.2.9), so generality across models/frameworks is unestablished. Reported numbers are approximate (~-prefixed, e.g. ~28k, ~125k) and appear to derive from a small number of runs with no variance, confidence intervals, or repetition counts reported; the emergent scripting workaround and the 'faulty' recovery cascade are somewhat anecdotal (e.g. the model calling the system 'fascinatingly complex'), reflecting non-deterministic agent behavior that may not reproduce. The three additional deployment vectors (input-token/SKILL.md bloat, context-history accumulation, execution-frequency amplification) and the two adaptive mechanisms (nonce-based repair, compaction-aware two-phase escalation) are proposed/analyzed but NOT empirically quantified -- explicitly deferred to future work. No defenses are evaluated; the paper only lists candidate mitigations (per-skill token budgets, SKILL.md size limits, privilege separation). The 5.7M-tokens-overnight figure is a cited community/non-malicious anecdote, not a controlled result. OpenClaw itself appears to be a fictional/renamed stand-in framework used for the study.

datasets_used: No standard ML dataset; evaluation uses a single identical user query ('Can you fetch the latest BBC headline?') against a benign vs. Trojanized OpenClaw 'query' skill
Target system: local OpenClaw v2026.2.9 deployment with Gemini 2.5 Pro backend via Google OAuth, standard tool suite (exec shell/Python, file-system, web search)
ClawHub community registry context: stated 5,700+ third-party installable skills
quantitative_results: Benign baseline: ~3.6k input, ~425 output, ~28k total context tokens, 1x, correct result
SVP v1 (L=50, T_max=2): ~25k input, ~14k output, ~125k context, ~6x amplification, correct result
SVP v2 (L=1000, T_max=3): ~25k input, ~14k output, ~190k context, ~7x amplification, correct result, completed in under 2 minutes
SVP v3 (L=2000, T_max=5): ~34k input, ~28k output, ~249k context, ~9x amplification, FAILED (no correct result), over 11 minutes wall-clock

Emergent scripting workaround collapsed per-turn output cost from ~1000 tokens to ~30 tokens (a shell command); autoregressive errors occurred at sequence positions 372 and 570 before the agent scripted around it
Theoretical per-query output cost of SVP: Omega(L x T_max) output tokens plus growing input history
Cited community anecdote: 5.7M tokens consumed overnight by a misconfigured (non-malicious) automation
baselines_compared: Benign query skill (same interface, no multi-turn protocol) as the in-paper 1x control baseline

Zhou et al. [25], 'Beyond Max Tokens' -- prior simulator-based work reporting 65-658x amplification with L up to 15,000, and using MCTS optimization plus ASR as a co-metric; used as the primary comparison/contrast point (cited work, not this paper's result)
Simulator-vs-deployment framing: prior constrained simulators restrict the agent to only the malicious tool, precluding the tool-composition behavior this paper observes
